intro: Transitioning from C++ to Python trades strict compile-time performance and manual memory management for rapid development velocity and a highly expressive syntax. Developers typically convert codebases to leverage Python's extensive data science ecosystem or to simplify complex multi-threaded logic into high-level asynchronous workflows. This shift shifts the focus from machine-level optimization to architectural productivity.

diffs.from: Statically typed with mandatory variable declarations, compile-time checking, and rigid object hierarchies.
diffs.to: Dynamically and strongly typed where types are associated with values at runtime, eliminating explicit type declarations.

diffs.from: Verbose, block-scoped syntax utilizing semicolons, explicit braces, and mandatory boilerplate like main functions.
diffs.to: Minimalist, indentation-scoped syntax where whitespace determines blocks, omitting semicolons and explicit wrappers for simple scripts.

diffs.from: Manual memory management via RAII, smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr), or raw new/delete operators.
diffs.to: Automated memory management driven by a reference-counting garbage collector supplemented by a cyclic garbage sweeper.

diffs.from: Low-level OS threads via std::thread, futures, promises, and synchronization primitives like std::mutex.
diffs.to: High-level event-loop driven concurrency using the async/await syntax and built-in libraries like asyncio.

diffs.from: The Standard Template Library (STL) focusing on foundational containers, algorithms, and basic input/output utilities.
diffs.to: A comprehensive "batteries-included" standard library containing rich modules for networking, file parsing, and system serialization.

examples.title: Filtering an Array/List
examples.description: Converting explicit template-based iterator filtering into idiomatic list comprehensions.
examples.toCode:
def filter_even(nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    return [n for n in nums if n % 2 == 0]

examples.title: Reading a File safely
examples.description: Replacing explicit RAII stream abstractions with context managers.

tips.title: Profile Before Porting
tips.body: Identify CPU-bound bottlenecks; hot paths reliant on raw cache-locality or SIMD operations will suffer significant performance drops in pure Python and may require C extensions.

tips.title: Adopt Type Hinting
tips.body: Mitigate the loss of compile-time safety by utilizing Python's typing module along with static analysis tools like mypy.

tips.title: Replace RAII with Context Managers
tips.body: Convert C++ scope-bound resource destructors directly into Python 'with' statements to ensure determinism in resource cleanup.

tips.title: Leverage Built-In Structures
tips.body: Avoid re-implementing basic search or sort algorithms; Python's internal dicts and lists are heavily optimized in C.

faqs.question: How do I replace C++ templates in Python?
faqs.answer: Python inherently handles this via duck typing, meaning functions accept any object that implements the required methods. For strict static checking, use TypeVar generics with typing hints.

faqs.question: Will my parallel code break due to Python's GIL?
faqs.answer: Yes, true multi-threaded CPU parallelization is constrained by Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL). You must pivot to the multiprocessing module or utilize C extensions to run parallel compute intensive operations.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of a pointer in Python?
faqs.answer: Python does not expose pointers or address arithmetic. Every variable is an object reference, meaning mutable structures passed to functions behave similarly to pointers or references.
